text: When we began our surgery, we noticed that the patient had easily identifiable left upper lobe superior division vein.
However, upon closer examination, this patient clearly had a common venous branching pattern.
The left upper lobe and left lower lobe venous branches could easily be identified and easily, in some cases, where a left upper lobectomy would be approached,
an inadvertent left upper lobectomy could be performed in patients with a common pulmonary vein.

Our surgery begins by going to the posterior aspect of the pleura, dividing the pleura along the medial aspect of the aorta, and separating the lymph nodes from the pulmonary artery at the top and the bronchus at the lower portion of the upper lobe boundary.
By taking the lymph nodes meticulously in this area and taking the station 7 lymph nodes, a mediastinal lymph node and a hilar lymphadenectomy is performed as well as exposing the hilum.
Extending the pleural division all the way around the top aspect of the pulmonary artery facilitates a perfect exposure.

Taking down the inferior pulmonary vein, taking care not to damage the inferior pulmonary vein attachments, but taking only the inferior pulmonary ligament and the lymph nodes along the inferior pulmonary ligament allows a thorough mediastinal dissection as you can see in this photograph and video.

Taking the lymph nodes off of the inferior pulmonary ligament, sparing the inferior pulmonary vein, and recognizing that there is a common pulmonary vein in this case will allow you to perform a safe and thorough surgery.
In circumstances where we're operating on the left, we sometimes place our anterior camera port, as we're using in this instance, slightly posterior to avoid the heart.

We then identify the pleura, taking it away from the venous structures, sweeping it away, and clearly identifying the S123 segmental anatomy by identifying the V123 venous branches first, specifically sparing the venous branches going to the lingula.
We use an endoscopic stapler gray load with a curve tip to provide the division of the superior division segmental vein.
This is taken with a single load and requires adequate hemostasis during the division of that vein.
By using the stapler and carefully watching as it's removed, we prevent unnecessary tension on the lobe during the division.
It's also important to recognize the left phrenic nerve, which needs to be preserved during the division of this portion of the surgery.

Again, taking the superior division pulmonary vein with a curved tip endogia stapler allows safe division of this vein and preservation of the lingular venous branches and preserves all of the other venous branches going to the remaining portion of the left upper lobe and of course the left lower lobe.
Always check for a patent left lower lobe venous branch before dividing.
Our attention will then be directed towards the first branch going to the superior division of the left upper lobe, which will be the A1 and A2 arterial branches.
By encircling, stapling, and dividing with the tan load curve tip endogia stapler, we can take those A1 and A2 branches going to the left upper lobe superior division pulmonary anatomy.
Once the A1 and A2 pulmonary artery branches have been taken with an endoscopic stapler, we then come across the contact with the bronchus.
The haptic feedback from the sucker tip pushing against the bronchus

confirms that it is not a vessel and is in fact bronchus and the lymphadenectomy by taking the lymph nodes at the branch points of the vessels and in between the bronchus and the artery you facilitate development of the entry point and the exit point using bovie electrocautery to prevent bleeding where the bronchial arteries are often lying just below the lymph nodes to facilitate
exposure and division of the concerned branches.

Two additional branches are taken as they have a late bifurcation with a common channel and a serrat is passed around these pulmonary artery branches to facilitate passage and development of the entry point and the exit point.
Sometimes pushing the serrat far behind the artery and using an
above the electrocautery to facilitate a little exit point will allow you to then pass and dilate that posterior passage.

By passing the serrat circumferentially around that subsequent pulmonary artery, you can then begin to encircle, staple, and divide with an endogia tan load stapler, just as you had done with those earlier branches going to the pulmonary artery at the beginning.

Taking care not to inadvertently damage any structures that cannot be visualized, it is always a good idea to visualize the posterior backside of the stapler and clearly see it prior to dividing, releasing tension and retraction on the lobe to facilitate division without having tension placed on the vessel as it is divided.
By continuing the division of the pulmonary artery branch, you can then begin to encircle, staple, and divide the S1, 2, and 3 branches of the left upper lobe B1, 2, and 3.
The bronchial branches going to the superior division of the left upper lobe of the lung facilitate the entry division and the posterior exit division by passing a sucker posterior to the bronchus.
an endogia purple load non-curve tip stapler is passed behind this area.

When I have learners in the room, I like to make sure that I close the stapler and occlude the segment that I intend to remove and ventilate the lung to show that the remaining portions of the lung inflate easily and that I am in fact preserving the lingula and not inadvertently taking the left upper lobe entirely and not inadvertently taking the lower lobe or any unintentional segments.
As the lingula is seen to begin to inflate, we then continue our left upper lobe stapling and attention is directed towards completing the lymphadenectomy.
By making sure that we preserve the lingular branches going to the left upper lobe and making sure that we preserve the lower lobe branches, we can easily complete our left upper lobe segmentectomy.
By taking the fissure with a purple load endogia, we can then complete
our fissure or intersegmental plane.

In some circumstances, the pinpoint system with ICG injection can be used to divide the intersegmental plane, and in other cases, a bovia electrocautery can be used to divide the intersegmental plane to provide maximal lung expansion and to provide preservation of the vein going to that specific segment.
In some cases where the tumor might be adjacent to a basilar segment intersegmental plane, a bisegmentectomy can be performed in the basilar region.

In this particular circumstance, we elected to perform a superior division segmentectomy because of the fact that we were concerned that this was a stage 1a lung cancer and otherwise good lung function was present within this patient.
We completed the fissure division with the purple load endogia stapler and re-expanded the lung to ensure that we were preserving both the lingula of the lung as well as the lower lobe.
By continuing this stapled fissure division, we ensured that we had in fact preserved all of the lung that we intended to.
Dividing the fissure along this plane then facilitated the exit from either a subxiphoid port or from the remaining utility port.
